content: As per IS 456:2000 provisions for two-way slabs with continuous edges, the longitudinal steel reinforcement to be provided in the edge strip is based on

options.content: the area of longitudinal steel provided in the middle strip in the shorter span
the area of longitudinal steel provided in the middle strip in the longer span
the prescribed minimum cross-sectional area of longitudinal steel for slabs

explanation:
As per IS 456 : 2000

In edge strip minimum reinforcement are provided.

$0.15 \%$ of BD for Fe250

$0.12 \%$ of BD for HYSD

content: A simply supported, uniformly loaded, two-way slab panel is torsionally unrestrained. The effective span lengths along the short span (x) and long span (y) directions of the panel are $l_{x}$ and $l_{y}$, respectively. The design moments for the reinforcements along the x and y directions are $M_{ux}$ and $M_{uy}$, respectively. By using Rankine-Grashoff method, the ratio $M_{ux}/M_{uy}$ is proportional to
explanation:
To determine the ratio $$\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{uy}}$$ for a simply supported, uniformly loaded, two-way slab panel using the Rankine-Grashoff method, we should understand the general principle behind the distribution of moments in two-way slabs.

The Rankine-Grashoff method indicates that the moments in the x and y directions (i.e., $$M_{ux}$$ and $$M_{uy}$$) for a uniformly loaded two-way slab panel are roughly proportional to the square of the span lengths in those directions. Mathematically, this can be expressed as:

$$M_{ux} \propto l_{y}^{2}$$

and

$$M_{uy} \propto l_{x}^{2}$$

Therefore, the ratio $$\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{uy}}$$ can be given as:

$$\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{uy}} = \frac{l_{y}^{2}}{l_{x}^{2}} = \left( \frac{l_{y}}{l_{x}} \right)^{2}$$

From this relationship, we can see that the ratio $$\frac{M_{ux}}{M_{uy}}$$ is proportional to $$\left( \frac{l_{y}}{l_{x}} \right)^{2}$$.

Thus, the correct option is:

Option D: $$(\frac{l_{y}}{l_{x}})^{2}$$

content: A slab panel with an effective depth of 250 mm is reinforced with 0.2% main reinforcement using 8 mm diameter steel bars. The uniform center-to-center spacing (in mm) at which the 8 mm diameter bars are placed in the slab panel is __________ (rounded off to the nearest integer).
explanation:
To determine the center-to-center spacing of the 8 mm diameter bars in the slab panel with a given reinforcement percentage, we'll start by calculating the required area of steel reinforcement. Here are the steps:

Step 1: Calculate the cross-sectional area of the slab

The cross-sectional area (Acs) of the slab per meter width is:

$$A_{cs} = \text{width} \times \text{effective depth} = 1000 \, \text{mm} \times 250 \, \text{mm} = 250000 \, \text{mm}^2$$

Step 2: Calculate the required area of steel reinforcement

The steel reinforcement percentage is 0.2%. Therefore, the area of steel reinforcement (Ast) required is:

$$A_{st} = 0.2\% \times A_{cs} = 0.002 \times 250000 \, \text{mm}^2 = 500 \, \text{mm}^2$$

Step 3: Calculate the area of one 8 mm diameter steel bar

The cross-sectional area (Abar) of one 8 mm diameter steel bar is:

$$A_{bar} = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (\text{diameter})^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (8 \, \text{mm})^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 64 \, \text{mm}^2 = 50.27 \, \text{mm}^2$$

Step 4: Calculate the number of bars per meter width

To find the number of bars (N) required per meter width of the slab:

$$N = \frac{A_{st}}{A_{bar}} = \frac{500 \, \text{mm}^2}{50.27 \, \text{mm}^2} \approx 9.95$$

We round it to the nearest integer, so we need approximately 10 bars per meter width.

Step 5: Calculate the center-to-center spacing

The spacing (S) between the bars can be calculated by dividing the width of the slab (1000 mm) by the number of bars plus one spacing gap:

$$S = \frac{1000 \, \text{mm}}{N} = \frac{1000 \, \text{mm}}{10} = 100 \, \text{mm}$$

Thus, the center-to-center spacing of the 8 mm diameter bars in the slab panel is 100 mm.

content: Consider a doubly reinforced RCC beam with the option of using either Fe250 plain bars or Fe500 deformed bars in the compression zone. The modulus of elasticity of steel is 2 × 10<sup>5</sup> N/mm<sup>2</sup> . As per IS456 : 2000, in which type(s) of the bars, the stress in the compression steel (f<sub>sc</sub>) can reach the design strength (0.87 f<sub>y</sub>) at the limit state of collapse?

options.content: Both Fe250 plain bars and Fe500 deformed bars
Neither Fe250 plain bars nor Fe500 deformed bars

explanation:
The strain in the compression zone, denoted as εsc, is calculated using the formula :

εsc = 0.0035 × $ \frac{Xu - dc}{dc}$

Therefore, εsc < 0.0035

However, we can consider εsc ≈ 0.0035 for practical purposes.

For Fe250:

The yield strain is calculated as $ \frac{0.87 \times 250}{200000}$ = 1.0875 × 10-3, which is less than 0.0035.

When εsc = 0.0035, fsc = 0.87 fy

For Fe500 :

The yield strain is calculated as $ \frac{0.87 \times 500}{200000} + 0.002$

So, $ \frac{0.87 \times 500}{200000} + 0.002$ = 4.175 × 10-3

Hence, fsc < 0.87 fy

Therefore, Fe250 will reach 0.87fy

Thus, only Fe250 plain bars are suitable.

content: The effective spans for a simple one-way slab system, with an overhang are indicated in the figure below. The specified ultimate design loads on the slab are $$6.0\,kN/{m^2}$$ and $$4.5\,kN/{m^2}$$ for dead loads and live loads respectively. Considering the possibility of live loads not occurring simultaneously on both spans, determine the maximum spacing (in $$mm$$ units) of $$8$$ $$mm$$ diameter bars required as bottom reinforcement in the span $$AB,$$ assuming an effective depth of $$125$$ $$mm.$$ Assume $$M20$$ concrete and $$Fe$$ $$415$$ steel.
